name: ketchup vinaigrette
instructions:
MACERATE: finely chop dill. Microplane garlic. Combine the chopped dill and microplaned garlic in red wine vinegar and let sit for ~15 minutes. The macerate reduces the dill's raw bitterness and infuses the vinegar with garlic — both are important here.

BUILD THE DRESSING: stir ketchup and dijon mustard into the macerated vinegar to taste. The ratio is to taste — generally heavier on ketchup than mustard, but adjust based on how sweet/acidic/sharp you want the final dressing.

FINISH: that's it. Pour over salad or use as a base for other recipes.
notes:
Built on a similar scaffolding to the tomato vinaigrette (samin nosrat) — vinegar maceration with aromatics is the structural move — but the rest of the ingredient set is shifted into american-cookout register (ketchup, mustard, dill).

WHY MACERATE THE DILL: not because dill is sharp or bitter — it isn't. The macerate is purely for infusion: the 15-minute sit pulls dill flavor into the vinegar where it can dissolve evenly into the dressing. Skipping the macerate leaves you with discrete dill bits floating in the dressing rather than dill-flavored vinegar binding the whole thing together.

WHY MACERATE THE GARLIC: same reason — raw microplaned garlic is aggressive, and the vinegar tames it during the 15-minute sit.

KETCHUP AND MUSTARD TO TASTE: this dressing has wide tolerance in the ketchup/mustard ratio. Heavier on ketchup gives you a sweeter, picnic-leaning dressing; heavier on mustard pulls it sharper and more savory. Adjust based on what salad it's going on.

RED ONION (OPTIONAL — TRICKIER THAN IT SOUNDS): red onion pairs beautifully with the ketchup-mustard-dill axis, but it doesn't macerate well in this dressing the way it does in tomato vinaigrette. Tomato vinaigrette has enough liquid volume (grated tomato pulp + vinegar) to hold a meaningful quantity of macerated shallot/onion; this dressing is more concentrated and doesn't have the liquid budget. If you want red onion in the dressing, macerate it SEPARATELY in vinegar first, then add — and use less than instinct suggests, because the dressing can't accommodate as much as you'd want. Or skip the dressing and add raw red onion to whatever salad this is going on.
